title: HEXGEN-FLOW: Optimizing LLM Inference Request Scheduling for Agentic Text-to-SQL
core_contribution: Two-level scheduler (global workload-balanced dispatch + local urgency-based priority queue with propagating SLO budgets) that upgrades LLM serving scheduling from per-request to per-workflow granularity for agentic multi-stage workloads on heterogeneous GPU clusters.
summary: HexGen-Flow targets agentic Text-to-SQL workloads (CHESS/MAC-SQL) where each user query triggers ~20 dependent LLM calls across 4 stages on heterogeneous GPU pools (A100+A6000+L40). Existing schedulers (vLLM FCFS+RR, VTC, QLM) treat LLM requests as independent tasks, ignoring stage dependencies, hardware heterogeneity, and end-to-end SLO propagation. HexGen-Flow introduces a hierarchical scheduler: a global coordinator dispatches each request via a non-linear score Score=(1-α)·β/t_queue - α·t_comp balancing task suitability against load; each instance runs a local priority queue ordered by urgency U=t_comp-(t_SLO-τ_queue), where per-stage SLO budgets are allocated proportionally to average compute cost and recomputed whenever an upstream stage completes (so upstream overruns automatically tighten downstream budgets). α is auto-tuned every 100s via a lightweight CPU simulator triggered by t-test-detected P95 regression. On BIRD-bench with Llama3.1-70B across three deployments, HexGen-Flow reduces P95 tail latency by 1.42-1.56x and improves throughput by 1.49-1.81x over vLLM/VTC/QLM; ablations confirm WB contributes 10.5-34.8% and PQ up to 48% P95 reduction; MAC-SQL+Qwen3-30B on Spider shows 34-38% P95 improvement, confirming generalization beyond Text-to-SQL.
key_findings: Workflow-aware SLO budget propagation (budgets reallocated on every stage completion) is the core primitive that makes per-request urgency metrics robust to estimation errors.

On heterogeneous GPUs, round-robin dispatching is actively harmful: case study shows RR sends 43.9% of stage-1 (light) requests to A100 while WB correctly routes 57.5% of stage-2 (heavy) requests to A100 instead.
Optimal α drifts with hardware and workload (Hetero-1: 0.1-0.2; Hetero-2: 0.3-0.4), mandating online auto-tuning; CPU simulator overhead is only 115-158s, negligible versus hour-scale workload drift.
WB and PQ are orthogonal and both necessary: neither alone matches the full WB+PQ system; WB alone reduces P95 by 10.5-34.8%, PQ alone by up to 48%.

Priority queue makes counter-intuitive decisions (selects request arriving at t=64.4s with urgency 26.9 over request arriving at t=22.4s with urgency 14.5) — this FCFS violation is what drives SLO attainment gains.
The scheduling framework generalizes beyond Text-to-SQL: swapping to MAC-SQL + Qwen3-30B on Spider traces still yields 34-38% P95 improvement.
limitations: Relies on accurate output length prediction (Zheng et al. 2023); no analysis of what prediction MAPE causes scheduling to collapse.
Assumes static workflow DAG — dynamic agentic workflows (e.g., LangGraph runtime stage expansion) break the proportional SLO budget allocation.
Preemption overhead (KV cache swap/evict in vLLM) is not separately profiled, so PQ's reported 48% P95 gain may be gross rather than net.
No comparison with modern multi-instance schedulers (Llumnix, DistServe, Mooncake) — only compared against weaker baselines (vLLM FCFS+RR, VTC, QLM).
α-tuning assumes workload stationarity over 100s windows; bursty traffic (flash sales, retry storms) can invalidate simulator trace.
Single centralized coordinator is a scaling bottleneck; no discussion of sharding for 100+ instance deployments.
Doesn't address prefill-decode disaggregation or prefix caching, both now standard in modern serving stacks.

infra_impact.algorithm: Enables productionization of inference-time scaling algorithms (best-of-N, self-correction, iterative refinement) by giving them SLO guarantees at the serving layer.
infra_impact.kernel: N/A — built atop vLLM, does not modify CUDA kernels or attention implementations.
infra_impact.framework: Direct blueprint for next-generation serving frameworks: vLLM/SGLang should expose workflow-DAG APIs and SLO budget propagation primitives so upper-layer agent frameworks can inject stage dependencies.
infra_impact.llm: N/A for model design; indirectly supports long-CoT and multi-step reasoning models by making their multi-call deployment tractable.
infra_impact.agent: Milestone for agentic LLM serving: first systematic workflow-aware scheduler. Reference implementation for AutoGen / LangGraph / CrewAI production deployments.
infra_impact.cluster: Targets enterprise heterogeneous GPU pools explicitly (A100 + A6000 + L40); complements cluster-level scheduling research like Llumnix by handling workflow-level orchestration.
infra_impact.code: Open source at github.com/Relaxed-System-Lab/Hexgen-Flow; usable as a reference agentic serving scheduler built on vLLM.

open_questions: How does HexGen-Flow compose with Llumnix's dynamic instance migration and DistServe's PD disaggregation — orthogonal gain or conflict?
At what output-length-prediction MAPE does the SLO budget allocation collapse, and can uncertainty-aware predictors (conformal prediction, Bayesian) improve robustness?
What is the net benefit of PQ preemption after accounting for KV cache eviction/swap overhead in vLLM?
How does the centralized coordinator scale to 100+ instances, and what sharding strategy preserves cross-instance workload visibility?
Can the workflow-aware scheduling primitive be extended to RLHF/GRPO training (rollout → reward → update as multi-stage workflow)?
How does the system handle dynamic agentic DAGs where runtime decisions add new stages (tool calls, branching reasoning)?
